preamble: The shift from a uvular fricative (voiced [ʁ] or voiceless [χ]) to the voiceless velar stop [k] is primarily observed in loanword adaptations and allophonic variation, where the posterior fricative is 'hardened' into a more common velar stop.

examples.note: Historical adaptation of the Greek posterior fricative into a velar stop in languages lacking uvular or velar fricatives (e.g., chaos, chronic).
Inverse allophonic shift where the velar stop is realized as a uvular fricative in specific contexts (e.g., before dark L).

sources: Dialectology studies on US English allophonic variation.
